title: Decompose pre-bash.py fat entry point into a guard library
evidence: pre-bash.py is a hook ENTRY POINT at 1,119 LOC with 25 top-level functions; `_run(root)` alone spans lines 765-1075 (~310 lines of sequential guard logic). coding-standards.md mandates: 'Shared logic goes in a leading-underscore library module ... Keep entry points thin; keep testable logic in the lib.' Every other guard follows this (pre-write/pre-edit/pre-read are thin over _hooklib); pre-bash accreted H-01/H-02/H-09b/H-10b/H-14, heredoc analysis, no-verify cluster parsing, exec-root resolution, and pathspec logic in the entry file. It is synced byte-identical to 3 hosts and is the branch's second-highest-churn file (13 changes since 06-20).
impact: The highest-value enforcement path is the hardest unit to review and test; each new guard grows one monolithic `_run`, raising regression risk across all three governance hosts simultaneously.
recommendation: Extract the bash-guard logic (command parsing, heredoc/executor detection, branch/tip checks, violation composition) into a `_bashguardlib.py` per the repo's own _*lib convention, leaving pre-bash.py as a thin stdin/exit-code shell; split `_run` into per-gate functions with H-NN-named units.
acceptance_criteria: pre-bash.py entry file under ~200 LOC with guard logic in a _*lib module
no single function exceeding ~80 lines in the extracted lib
existing pre-bash tests pass unchanged against the lib API
